# Atorvastatin and Cardiovascular Mortality: A Randomized Controlled Trial

## Abstract

This randomized controlled trial (RCT) evaluated the effect of atorvastatin 40mg daily on cardiovascular mortality in 4,200 patients with moderate hypercholesterolemia over a 5-year follow-up period.

## Methods

Patients aged 45-75 with LDL cholesterol >130 mg/dL were randomized 1:1 to atorvastatin 40mg daily or placebo. The primary endpoint was cardiovascular mortality. Secondary endpoints included major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) and all-cause mortality.

## Results

Atorvastatin significantly reduced cardiovascular mortality compared to placebo (HR 0.72, 95% CI 0.61-0.85, p<0.001). The treatment group showed a 28% relative risk reduction in cardiovascular death. All-cause mortality was also reduced (HR 0.85, 95% CI 0.74-0.97).

LDL cholesterol decreased by 42% in the atorvastatin group versus 2% in the placebo group. The number needed to treat (NNT) to prevent one cardiovascular death over 5 years was 34.

Adverse events were similar between groups, with myalgia reported in 8.2% of the atorvastatin group versus 7.1% in placebo (p=0.23). Serious hepatotoxicity occurred in 0.3% of statin-treated patients.

## Discussion

This RCT of 4,200 patients demonstrates that atorvastatin 40mg daily provides a clinically significant reduction in cardiovascular mortality. These findings support current guidelines recommending statin therapy for patients with moderate-to-high cardiovascular risk.

## Conclusion

Atorvastatin therapy is associated with positive outcomes for cardiovascular mortality reduction in patients with hypercholesterolemia. The benefit-risk profile supports its use as first-line therapy.
